nativeRange.habitat: Seaward reefs at depths of 5–35 m, among live rock and coral structures

diet.notes: In the wild, has been documented consuming mollusks, crustaceans, worms, sea urchins, and echinoderms. In captivity, generally accepts vitamin-enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, chopped seafood, and high-quality marine pellet foods. Feeding 2–3 times daily is generally advised, especially during the acclimation period.

summary: The Harlequin Tusk is a boldly banded Indo-Pacific wrasse (Choerodon fasciatus) recognizable by its distinctive blue teeth. Generally considered semi-aggressive, it is best kept singly in a large aquarium and tends to consume small crustaceans while typically leaving corals undisturbed.
careNotes:
The Harlequin Tusk is a large, bold wrasse with alternating red, white, blue, and black body bands and characteristic bright blue tusks — used in the wild to crack open hard-shelled invertebrate prey. A minimum aquarium of 500 litres (around 150 cm in length) is generally advised to give an adult adequate territory; smaller tanks have been associated with increased aggression and difficulty acclimating. It is generally advised to keep only one specimen per tank, as they are widely considered highly aggressive toward conspecifics. Juveniles tend to be shy and reclusive when first introduced, but typically gain confidence and become bolder as they mature. Most specimens will rearrange rockwork over time, so securing rock structures firmly is generally advised to prevent collapses.

Reef compatibility is somewhat nuanced: the species has been reported to leave corals and clams largely undisturbed, but tends to consume small crustaceans reliably — including decorative shrimp, snails, hermit crabs, and similar invertebrates. For this reason it is commonly considered safe for reef systems only with caution, and is better suited to a fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) setup where ornamental invertebrates are not kept.

The Harlequin Tusk is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning females can transition to male when a dominant male is absent. Wild specimens are found in pairs or small groups on seaward reefs, though in captivity they are best kept singly due to territorial behavior.

Australian-sourced specimens are commonly considered hardier and more adaptable in captivity than Philippine-sourced individuals, though this is based on anecdotal hobbyist observation rather than controlled study.
breedingNotes: The Harlequin Tusk is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning females are capable of transitioning to male when dominant males are absent from the group. Captive breeding has not been documented in the aquarium hobby. In the wild the species is oviparous (egg-laying), with spawning occurring in distinct male-female pairs.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite. No documented captive breeding; wild spawning involves distinct pairing.
